title: Brick towers
description:
Young Peter has got a wonderful gift for his birthday --- the whole pack of wooden bricks. He has already made $n$ towers, with heights $a_1$, $a_2$, $\ldots$, $a_n$ bricks, correspondingly.

Peter's favorite number is $k$, so he likes towers with height exactly $k$ bricks. Peter finds a set of consecutive towers good if their average height is equal to exactly $k$ bricks. He wants to find a good set of towers containing as many consecutive towers as possible.

Help Peter to find the largest good set of towers.
input: The first line of input file contains two integer numbers $n$ and $k$ ($1 \le n \le 100000$; $1 \le k \le 10^9$). The second line of input file contains $n$ integer numbers $a_i$ ($1 \le a_i \le 10^9$) --- heights of the towers.
output: Output two integer numbers $l$ and $m$ --- amount of towers in the largest good set and number of the first tower in this set. Towers are numbered from $1$ in order of appearance in the input file. If there are multiple answers, output any of them. If there is no good set output single number $0$.
source: Olympiad, Russian Olympiad in Informatics, Internet Olympiads in Informatics, 2010-2011 Season, February 11, 2011
